questions.questionText: Bioavailability of a drug given intravenously is:
questions.explanation: IV administration: 100% bioavailability (drug directly enters systemic circulation, bypassing absorption barriers). Oral bioavailability is always less due to incomplete absorption and first-pass metabolism (hepatic). Bioavailability (F) = (AUC oral / AUC IV) × 100. Factors reducing oral bioavailability: first-pass effect (liver metabolism), poor GI absorption, drug degradation in gut, P-glycoprotein efflux. Reference: KD Tripathi — Pharmacokinetics.

questions.optionB: Metabolism of a drug by the liver (and gut wall) BEFORE it reaches systemic circulation after oral administration
questions.explanation: First-pass metabolism (presystemic metabolism): after oral absorption, drug passes through portal circulation → liver → extensive hepatic metabolism before reaching systemic circulation → reduced bioavailability. Drugs with high first-pass: nitroglycerin (given sublingual to bypass), morphine, propranolol, lidocaine (given IV only), isoprenaline. Routes that bypass first-pass: IV, sublingual, transdermal, rectal (lower 1/3 → IVC), inhalational. Reference: KD Tripathi — First-Pass Effect.

questions.optionA: Rate of elimination is proportional to drug concentration
questions.optionB: A CONSTANT amount of drug is eliminated per unit time (regardless of plasma concentration)
questions.explanation: Zero-order kinetics: constant amount eliminated/time (rate is independent of drug concentration — metabolic enzymes are saturated). Half-life changes with concentration. Examples: ethanol (ADH saturation), phenytoin (at therapeutic doses — Michaelis-Menten/saturation kinetics), aspirin (high doses). First-order: constant FRACTION eliminated/time (rate proportional to concentration), half-life is constant. Most drugs follow first-order kinetics at therapeutic doses. Reference: KD Tripathi — Kinetics.

questions.optionB: Apparent volume into which the drug distributes in the body (Vd = Dose/Plasma concentration)
questions.explanation: Vd: theoretical volume that would contain the total drug in the body at the same concentration as in plasma. Vd = Dose (IV) / C0 (initial plasma concentration). Low Vd (~3-5L): drug stays in plasma (e.g., warfarin — highly protein-bound). High Vd (>40L): drug distributes extensively into tissues (e.g., chloroquine ~13000L, digoxin). Vd does NOT correspond to a real physiological volume. Important for calculating loading dose: LD = Vd × Css. Reference: KD Tripathi — Volume of Distribution.

questions.questionText: A drug with a narrow therapeutic index means:
questions.optionB: Small difference between therapeutic and toxic doses — requires careful monitoring
questions.explanation: Therapeutic index (TI) = TD50/ED50 (or LD50/ED50). Narrow TI: small margin between effective and toxic doses → requires therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM). Examples: warfarin, digoxin, lithium, phenytoin, aminoglycosides, theophylline, cyclosporine, methotrexate, carbamazepine. Wide TI: large safety margin (e.g., penicillin, diazepam). TDM involves measuring drug plasma levels to maintain within therapeutic window. Reference: KD Tripathi — Therapeutic Index.

questions.optionB: Oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis reactions (functionalization — primarily by cytochrome P450 in liver)
questions.explanation: Phase I (functionalization): introduces or unmasks a functional group (-OH, -NH2, -SH) via oxidation (most common — CYP450), reduction, and hydrolysis. CYP450 enzymes: liver microsomal, CYP3A4 (most important, metabolizes ~50% of drugs). Phase II (conjugation): attaches large polar group to Phase I product — glucuronidation (most common), sulfation, acetylation, methylation, glutathione conjugation. Not all drugs undergo Phase I before Phase II (e.g., morphine undergoes direct glucuronidation). Reference: KD Tripathi — Drug Metabolism.

questions.optionB: Rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, St. John's wort (mnemonic: 'Rif PCB SJW')
questions.explanation: CYP450 inducers: increase enzyme synthesis → faster drug metabolism → DECREASED plasma levels of substrates. Major inducers: rifampin (most potent), phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, St. John's wort, chronic alcohol. Clinical significance: rifampin reduces efficacy of OCP (contraceptive failure), warfarin, cyclosporine, protease inhibitors. CYP450 inhibitors: ketoconazole, erythromycin/clarithromycin, cimetidine, grapefruit juice, ritonavir → INCREASED plasma levels → toxicity. Reference: KD Tripathi — CYP450 Interactions.

questions.optionB: Decreased response to a drug with repeated administration, requiring higher doses for the same effect
questions.explanation: Tolerance: gradual decrease in drug response with repeated use → need higher dose for same effect. Types: pharmacokinetic (metabolic — enzyme induction), pharmacodynamic (receptor downregulation/desensitization), behavioral (learned adaptation). Tachyphylaxis: rapid tolerance (minutes-hours, e.g., ephedrine — indirect sympathomimetic, nitrates). Cross-tolerance: tolerance to one drug extends to related drugs (opioids, benzodiazepines). Tolerance ≠ dependence (but often coexist). Reference: KD Tripathi — Tolerance.

questions.questionText: Steady-state concentration is achieved after approximately:
questions.explanation: Steady-state (Css): rate of drug input = rate of elimination. Reached after ~4-5 half-lives (regardless of dose or frequency). After 1 t½: 50%, 2 t½: 75%, 3 t½: 87.5%, 4 t½: 93.75%, 5 t½: ~97% of Css. Loading dose is used when rapid attainment of therapeutic levels is needed (bypasses the wait). LD = Vd × Css. Maintenance dose = Css × clearance. Same 4-5 half-lives rule for drug washout (elimination after stopping). Reference: KD Tripathi — Steady State.

questions.questionText: An agonist with high efficacy and high affinity is termed a:
questions.explanation: Full agonist: binds receptor, has maximum efficacy (produces maximal response — Emax = 100%). Partial agonist: binds receptor, has SUBMAXIMAL efficacy (e.g., pindolol, buprenorphine) — acts as agonist alone but as antagonist in presence of full agonist (competes for receptor). Antagonist: binds receptor, zero efficacy (blocks agonist). Inverse agonist: produces response opposite to agonist (decreases constitutive receptor activity). Reference: KD Tripathi — Receptor Pharmacology.
